title: Croquetas de Jamón
subtitle: Spanish ham croquettes
excerpt: A thick béchamel packed with finely diced serrano ham, chilled until firm, then shaped, crumbed, and fried to a crackling golden crust with a molten, creamy centre.
sourceNotes: Croquetas de jamón are a cornerstone of Spanish tapas and home cooking. Documented in Penelope Casas, Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985) and Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011). The defining feature is the very thick, soft béchamel base, far beyond the stiffness of French croquettes.

content.text: The secret to a good croqueta is the béchamel. It must be much thicker than anything you would make for a lasagne or cauliflower cheese, stiff enough to hold its shape when cold, but still supple and creamy when bitten into hot. The ratio here is 1:1:8 (butter to flour to milk by weight), and the mixture needs 10 full minutes of stirring over gentle heat.

Chilling time is not optional. The béchamel needs at least 3 hours in the fridge, preferably overnight, to firm up enough to shape. Cold hands and a light touch help when forming the croquettes; they should be the shape and size of a large cork.

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: taste before adding, the ham is salty

content.text: Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium-low heat. Add the flour and stir constantly for 2 minutes to cook out the raw flour taste. Add the warm milk in a thin stream, stirring continuously. Cook, stirring, for a further 10 minutes until very thick, the spoon should leave a trail that holds for a moment. Add the ham, nutmeg, and salt. Stir to combine.

Pour the mixture into a shallow tray or container. Press cling film directly onto the surface to prevent a skin forming. Refrigerate for at least 3 hours, or overnight. The mixture should be firm enough to scoop and shape.

Set up three bowls: flour, beaten egg, and breadcrumbs. With lightly floured hands, scoop about 30 g of mixture, shape into a small log, and roll sequentially in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs. Set on a tray. Heat the oil to 175°C in a deep saucepan. Fry the croquettes in batches of 4 to 5 for 2 to 3 minutes until deep golden brown. Drain on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.fix: Fry at 175°C rather than higher; ensure the filling is fully firm before shaping
items.cause: Oil too hot, or béchamel not chilled long enough

items.fix: Dust thoroughly with flour first, shake off excess, then dip in egg and crumbs
items.cause: Not enough flour in the first coating; the flour is what the egg sticks to

items.fix: Stir over medium-low heat for the full 10 minutes until the mixture pulls away from the pan sides cleanly
items.cause: Not cooked long enough after the milk is added

content.text: Croquetas de jamón appear on every tapas menu in Spain. They are also made in every family kitchen, usually with leftover serrano ham or the ends of a sliced leg. The croquette technique arrived in Spain from France in the eighteenth century, via royal court cooking. But the Spanish version is much softer and creamier than the French original. It has become something entirely its own.
